Explanation: cuDNN can choose among multiple convolution algorithms, and with `torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = True`, PyTorch may time several algorithms on the first iteration (or when shapes change) and pick the fastest for those shapes. Small timing noise, different workspace availability, or slightly different execution conditions can lead to different chosen algorithms across runs, causing step-time variability (and sometimes nondeterminism if the chosen algorithm is nondeterministic).

Mitigations: (1) For stable performance, keep input shapes static and optionally keep `cudnn.benchmark=True` so it picks once and stays stable for a fixed shape; avoid dynamic shapes that trigger re-benchmarking. (2) For reproducibility, set `torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False` and enable deterministic behavior (`torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True` and/or `torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True)`), understanding this may reduce performance. (3) Ensure sufficient workspace/memory; lack of workspace can force slower algorithms.
questions.explanation: This tests practical understanding: cuDNN isn’t a single kernel. Algorithm choice depends on shapes, dtype, workspace, and flags. `benchmark` can improve speed but can introduce run-to-run variability and can select nondeterministic algorithms unless constrained. The mitigation is choosing appropriate flags and stabilizing shapes/workspace.

questions.question_text:
You’re optimizing a CNN training loop. You switch input tensors and model weights to channels-last and see a big speedup on an A100.

Which explanation best matches what’s happening in practice for many conv workloads?
options: A. Channels-last always reduces FLOPs in convolution, so it is always faster regardless of hardware.
B. Channels-last changes memory layout to improve coalescing and enable more efficient Tensor Core / cuDNN kernels for NHWC, often increasing effective bandwidth and compute utilization.
C. Channels-last forces cuDNN to use deterministic algorithms, which are faster.
D. Channels-last only helps on CPUs; on GPUs it usually slows down because of extra transposes.
questions.explanation: Many cuDNN convolution kernels are highly tuned for specific layouts. On modern NVIDIA GPUs, NHWC (channels-last) can map better to vectorized loads/stores and Tensor Core-friendly data access patterns, improving memory coalescing and enabling faster kernels. The speedup is not from fewer FLOPs but from better kernel selection and higher hardware utilization. It’s not universally faster (depends on shapes, dtype, and surrounding ops), but it’s a common practical knob for CV workloads.

questions.question_text:
You notice a small but consistent accuracy regression (e.g., validation metric drops slightly) after moving training from a V100 to an A100, even though you kept the same code and still use FP32 training (no AMP). You suspect TF32.

What are practical steps in PyTorch to (1) test whether TF32 is the cause, and (2) decide when to enable/disable TF32? Include the expected speed vs accuracy tradeoff and at least one concrete setting change.
questions.correct_answer:
To test TF32 as the cause: run an A/B experiment with TF32 explicitly disabled and compare metrics and loss curves. In PyTorch, set `torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = False` and `torch.backends.cudnn.allow_tf32 = False` (and optionally `torch.set_float32_matmul_precision('highest')` in newer versions) and rerun. If the regression disappears, TF32 is likely contributing.

When to enable TF32: enable when you want faster FP32 matmul/conv on Ampere+ and the model is tolerant to small numeric changes (many CV and large transformer workloads are). Expect noticeable speedups on GEMM/conv-heavy workloads because Tensor Cores can be used for FP32-ish math.

When to disable TF32: disable for workloads sensitive to small numeric differences (some scientific/physics models, certain normalization-heavy or ill-conditioned problems), for strict reproducibility comparisons across hardware, or when debugging numeric issues. Disabling TF32 can reduce throughput (sometimes substantially) but improves numerical fidelity closer to true FP32.

Decision approach: quantify the speed gain (throughput, step time) vs the metric impact; keep TF32 on if the metric impact is negligible and performance wins matter, otherwise turn it off for the sensitive parts (or globally).
questions.explanation: The key is that TF32 can change results even in “FP32 training” because it changes the math used in certain kernels (matmul/conv) on Ampere+. The most common, interview-relevant practice is to toggle the backend flags and compare. The tradeoff is typically faster training/inference for FP32 GEMM/conv at the cost of slightly reduced precision; many models tolerate it, but some don’t. A disciplined A/B run is the practical way to decide.

questions.question_text:
In PyTorch on an NVIDIA Ampere (or newer) GPU, you train a model with FP32 weights/activations and use `torch.cuda.amp.autocast()` (mixed precision). What is TF32 and what is PyTorch’s default behavior regarding TF32 for matmul/conv on Ampere+?

Choose the most accurate statement.
options: A. TF32 is a storage format that halves FP32 memory bandwidth; PyTorch always enables it for all FP32 ops on all GPUs.

B. TF32 is a Tensor Core math mode that uses FP32 range with ~10-bit mantissa; on Ampere+ PyTorch may use TF32 for FP32 matmul/conv (Tensor Core paths) depending on backend flags, even when your tensors are FP32.
C. TF32 is identical to FP16 compute but accumulates in FP32; PyTorch enables it only inside `autocast()`.
D. TF32 is an INT8 quantization mode; PyTorch enables it only when you call `torch.quantization` APIs.
questions.explanation: TF32 (TensorFloat-32) is a Tensor Core math mode introduced with NVIDIA Ampere: it keeps FP32 exponent range but reduces mantissa precision (roughly 10-bit) for the multiply, typically accumulating in FP32. This can significantly speed up FP32 GEMMs and many convolutions by using Tensor Cores while keeping FP32-like dynamic range. In PyTorch, TF32 usage is controlled by backend flags (commonly `torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32` and `torch.backends.cudnn.allow_tf32`) and can be used even when tensors are FP32 because it’s a compute mode, not a tensor dtype. `autocast()` is about choosing lower-precision dtypes (FP16/BF16) for some ops; TF32 is separate and targets FP32 ops.

questions.question_text: You enable `torch.compile(model)` and see performance regress or become unstable (frequent recompiles). Which is the MOST likely cause in real training code, and what is a practical fix?
options: A. Graph breaks or shape polymorphism issues (changing tensor shapes/control flow) causing recompilation; fix by making shapes more static, reducing Python-side data-dependent control flow, or using dynamic shape support carefully
B. torch.compile disables GPU execution and runs on CPU; fix by moving model to CUDA
C. torch.compile forces determinism and therefore must be slower; fix by disabling determinism
D. torch.compile always uses float64 internally; fix by casting back to float16
questions.explanation: In practice, `torch.compile` can suffer when the program has data-dependent Python control flow, varying shapes (e.g., variable sequence lengths), or ops that cause graph breaks. That can trigger recompilation and overhead that outweighs fusion benefits. Fixes include padding/bucketing to stabilize shapes, avoiding Python-side branching on tensor values, and configuring compilation/dynamic shapes appropriately.

questions.question_text: You are training a transformer and want higher throughput without running out of GPU memory. Compare these options: (1) increase batch size, (2) use gradient accumulation (microbatching), (3) reduce sequence length. When does each help most, and what trade-offs should you expect (throughput vs latency vs memory)?
questions.correct_answer:
1) Increasing batch size often improves throughput by increasing GPU utilization and amortizing overhead, but increases activation memory roughly proportional to batch and can increase end-to-end latency per step.

2) Gradient accumulation (microbatching) increases the effective batch size while keeping per-microbatch memory smaller, often improving throughput compared to tiny batches, but it increases step latency (multiple forward/backward passes before an optimizer step) and adds some overhead; it can also change optimizer dynamics if not handled carefully (e.g., scaling LR, handling dropout/BN).

3) Reducing sequence length reduces both compute and memory significantly for transformers (attention is often O(L^2) in compute/memory for full attention), frequently the most powerful lever when sequence length is the bottleneck. Trade-off: potentially worse model quality or task fit, and it changes the problem.

Rule of thumb: if you’re underutilizing the GPU and memory allows, increase batch; if memory is the limit, use accumulation; if sequence length dominates memory/compute, reducing L (or using more efficient attention) yields the biggest gains.
questions.explanation: These are the common ML-engineer levers for GPU efficiency. Batch size affects parallelism and launch amortization; accumulation trades latency for memory; sequence length is a dominant term in transformer cost and is often the real bottleneck.

questions.question_text:
Consider this timing code:

```python
import time, torch
x = torch.randn(4096, 4096, device='cuda', dtype=torch.float16)
w = torch.randn(4096, 4096, device='cuda', dtype=torch.float16)

t0 = time.time()
y = x @ w
print('ms:', (time.time() - t0) * 1000)
```

Why is this timing likely wrong, and how would you fix it (show corrected code)? Mention at least one common implicit synchronization footgun.
questions.correct_answer:
GPU ops are asynchronous w.r.t. the host, so `time.time()` measures only CPU enqueue time unless you synchronize. Fix by synchronizing (or using CUDA events) around the region. Also note that `print(y)`, `y.item()`, and transferring tensors to CPU (`y.cpu()`, `.numpy()`) can implicitly synchronize and distort measurements.

Corrected example using CUDA events:
```python
import torch
x = torch.randn(4096, 4096, device='cuda', dtype=torch.float16)
w = torch.randn(4096, 4096, device='cuda', dtype=torch.float16)

start = torch.cuda.Event(enable_timing=True)
end = torch.cuda.Event(enable_timing=True)

# warmup
for _ in range(10):
    _ = x @ w

torch.cuda.synchronize()
start.record()
y = x @ w
end.record()
torch.cuda.synchronize()
print('ms:', start.elapsed_time(end))
```
questions.explanation: Asynchrony is a major GPU footgun: the CPU can continue while the GPU runs. Proper benchmarking either synchronizes explicitly or uses CUDA events (which measure on-GPU time). Avoid accidental sync points inside the timed region.

questions.question_text: You want to check whether your matmuls are using Tensor Cores on an NVIDIA GPU. Which condition is generally required to reliably hit Tensor Core-accelerated kernels in deep learning frameworks?
options: A. Use supported dtypes (fp16/bf16/TF32 depending on GPU and settings) and Tensor Core-friendly shapes/alignments; otherwise you may fall back to SIMT cores
B. Tensor Cores are always used for any matmul on GPU regardless of dtype and shape
C. Tensor Cores only work when you disable cuDNN and use custom kernels
D. Tensor Cores require float64 inputs for accuracy
questions.explanation: Tensor Core usage depends on dtype and kernel selection, and often benefits from dimensions that are multiples of certain tile sizes (framework-dependent). If dtype/shape/layout don’t match, libraries may choose non–Tensor Core kernels. Tensor Cores are not universal; cuDNN/cuBLAS are typically the path to Tensor Cores; float64 is not a Tensor Core fast path on most GPUs.

questions.question_text: You profile a training step and see the GPU has frequent idle gaps between kernels, while CPU time shows many small ops and Python overhead. What is the most actionable next step for an ML engineer to reduce the gaps?
options: A. Increase GPU clock speed; idle gaps are always due to slow GPU hardware
B. Use operator fusion / torch.compile (or reduce Python-level overhead) to cut the number of launches and CPU dispatch time
C. Convert parameters to float64 so each kernel does more work
D. Disable asynchronous execution so the GPU waits less
questions.explanation: Idle gaps often mean the GPU is waiting for CPU to launch the next kernel or for sync points. Reducing Python overhead and fusing ops (e.g., torch.compile) can reduce kernel launch count and CPU dispatch latency, increasing GPU utilization. Float64 generally slows compute; disabling async makes things worse; clock speed doesn’t fix CPU-side launch bottlenecks.

questions.question_text: In mixed precision training, when is bf16 often safer than fp16 on GPUs, and why? Provide a practical rule of thumb and one example operation/pattern where it matters.
questions.correct_answer: bf16 is often safer when overflow/underflow risk is high because it has the same exponent range as fp32 (but fewer mantissa bits). Rule of thumb: prefer bf16 when activations/gradients can have large dynamic range (e.g., large logits, long reductions, some normalization/attention patterns) and you want fewer inf/NaN issues without heavy loss-scaling tuning. Example: attention logits/softmax or large-magnitude activations in transformers—fp16 can overflow in exp or intermediate products more easily; bf16 reduces overflow risk while still accelerating on modern GPUs.
questions.explanation: fp16’s narrower exponent range is the common failure mode (overflow to inf, underflow to zero). bf16 trades precision (mantissa) for range (exponent), which often makes training more stable in practice on supported hardware.

questions.question_text: You see NaNs during mixed-precision training, and you already use loss scaling. Which explanation and fix is most appropriate specifically for GPU mixed-precision behavior (beyond just "increase loss scale")?
options: A. Softmax can overflow in fp16; use a numerically stable softmax (subtract max) and/or compute softmax/logsumexp in fp32
B. The GPU cannot represent NaNs in fp16; switch to CPU
C. NaNs only come from bad data; GPUs do not introduce numerical issues
D. Set torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = False; TF32 always creates NaNs
questions.explanation: fp16 has a limited exponent range; exponentials in softmax/logsumexp can overflow easily, creating inf then NaNs. A stable softmax subtracts the max before exp, and many frameworks upcast critical reductions to fp32. NaNs are representable in fp16; GPU math/parallel reductions can expose numerical instability; TF32 typically affects precision, not usually NaN generation by itself.

questions.question_text: You need bitwise-identical training runs on a single GPU (same seed, same code). In PyTorch, which combination is MOST likely to be required (even if it slows training)?
options: A. Set seeds only (torch.manual_seed, numpy seed) and leave everything else default
B. Enable deterministic algorithms (torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True)), set CUDNN deterministic/benchmark flags appropriately, and avoid/replace known nondeterministic ops
C. Switch to float64 everywhere; determinism is guaranteed in float64
D. Use DataLoader with more workers to stabilize input order
questions.explanation: GPU nondeterminism often comes from algorithm choices (e.g., cuDNN selecting different kernels), atomic reductions, and parallel reduction order. PyTorch’s deterministic mode plus cuDNN settings can force deterministic kernels or error on nondeterministic ops, but may reduce performance. Float64 does not guarantee determinism; more DataLoader workers can make ordering less deterministic unless carefully controlled.

questions.question_text:
Many deep learning ops (e.g., layernorm, softmax, embedding lookups) are often described as "memory-bandwidth bound" on GPUs. Which statement best explains why?

Assume the kernels are reasonably optimized.
options: A. These ops have low arithmetic intensity (few FLOPs per byte), so performance is limited by moving data from HBM/cache rather than compute throughput
B. These ops always run on Tensor Cores, which saturate compute and therefore become bandwidth bound
C. These ops are slow because GPUs cannot do floating point division efficiently
D. These ops are slow because the GPU cannot overlap memory accesses with computation
questions.explanation: Bandwidth-bound kernels do relatively little computation per element loaded/stored. Even if the GPU has enormous compute throughput, it can’t exceed the rate at which it can read/write data from HBM (and through caches). Tensor Cores are not always used for these ops; division isn’t the dominant factor; and GPUs can overlap memory and compute, but low arithmetic intensity still caps throughput.

questions.question_text: Explain (at an ML-engineer level) why branchy or irregular control flow can make a GPU kernel slower even if the total number of operations is the same. Use the concepts of warps/SIMT and divergence; no CUDA coding required.
questions.correct_answer: GPUs execute threads in groups (warps) using SIMT: threads in a warp share an instruction stream. If threads take different branches (divergence), the warp must serialize the different paths (masking off threads not taking that path), reducing effective parallelism and wasting cycles. Irregular control flow also harms memory coalescing and makes it harder to keep many warps ready to run, which can lower occupancy/latency hiding. Net effect: same nominal ops, but fewer threads doing useful work per cycle and more stalls.
questions.explanation: The key intuition is that a warp is the scheduling unit. Divergence turns parallel execution into partial serialization. Occupancy matters because GPUs hide memory/pipe latency by switching between ready warps; divergence and irregularity can reduce how many warps are ready and how efficiently they execute.

questions.question_text:
You move a small PyTorch model from CPU to GPU and see it get slower for batch_size=1 inference. Which change is usually the FIRST thing to try to improve throughput without changing the model?

(Assume the GPU is otherwise idle.)
options: A. Increase batch size (or use micro-batching) to amortize launch and transfer overheads
B. Convert all weights to float64 to reduce numerical error
C. Disable cuDNN and force deterministic algorithms
D. Add more Python threads around the forward pass
questions.explanation: For tiny batches, GPU kernel launch overhead, CPU↔GPU synchronization, and memory-transfer overhead can dominate, making GPU slower than CPU. The most practical first lever is to increase effective batch size (batching or micro-batching) so each launch does more work. Float64 typically slows GPU compute; deterministic modes often reduce performance; Python threading rarely helps GPU-bound work and can add overhead.

questions.question_text: Distributed performance debugging (DDP/FSDP): You see per-iteration time spike and `nvidia-smi` shows GPUs periodically dropping to low utilization. Profiling shows long NCCL all-reduce times. Give a practical debugging approach to distinguish compute vs communication bottlenecks and list 3 concrete mitigations (including one about overlap and one about bucket sizing).
questions.correct_answer:
Approach:
- Measure iteration breakdown: forward, backward, optimizer, and comm. Use `torch.profiler` with NCCL events (and/or Nsight Systems) to see whether all-reduce overlaps with backward compute or appears as a tail (non-overlapped).
- Check scaling efficiency: compare single-GPU step time vs multi-GPU; if compute stays similar but total grows with world size and comm dominates, it’s comm-bound.
- Inspect NCCL/links: verify interconnect (NVLink/IB) and topology; check that you’re not accidentally using PCIe-only paths; verify correct NCCL env and that GPUs are on the expected fabric.
- Look for symptoms of poor overlap: all-reduce occurring after backward finishes (a big tail), or frequent sync points (e.g., `.item()`, CPU logging) preventing overlap.
Mitigations:
1) Improve overlap: ensure gradient bucketing/overlap is enabled (DDP default), avoid sync points in the training step, and consider `no_sync()` only when appropriate. For FSDP, tune prefetching/overlap knobs.
2) Bucket sizing: tune `ddp_bucket_cap_mb` (or equivalent) so buckets are large enough to amortize latency but not so large that they delay the start of all-reduce (hurting overlap). Too small → many all-reduces (latency/launch overhead); too large → late start and big tail.
3) Reduce communicated bytes: use gradient compression/low precision (where acceptable), use ZeRO/FSDP sharding to reduce per-rank state, or reduce model size / use activation checkpointing to allow larger microbatches (sometimes improving compute/comm ratio).
Additional mitigations:
- Increase batch size/microbatch to raise compute/comm ratio (if memory allows).
- Ensure fused optimizers/kernels to shorten backward and improve overlap window.
- For NCCL, set correct env for IB/NVLink, and ensure process affinity/NUMA pinning to avoid host bottlenecks.
questions.explanation: Distributed slowdowns often come from (a) communication volume, (b) poor overlap (comm becomes a tail), or (c) topology/config issues. The key is to observe whether NCCL runs concurrently with backward compute. Bucket sizing is a classic lever: it trades off early-start overlap vs latency overhead from many small collectives. Avoiding sync points is crucial because they can serialize the pipeline and eliminate overlap.

questions.question_text: Training vs inference: For a transformer, which statement best explains why training memory is often much higher than inference, and when gradient accumulation helps vs hurts?
options: A) Training uses fp32 but inference uses fp16; gradient accumulation always reduces total memory and always speeds up training.

B) Training stores activations for backward and also holds gradients + optimizer state; inference can often stream with minimal saved activations and may use KV cache. Gradient accumulation reduces per-step activation memory (smaller microbatches) but can increase total wall time and may increase optimizer/gradient residency time or reduce overlap depending on implementation.
C) Training memory is higher only due to dataloader prefetch; gradient accumulation mainly affects CPU RAM.
D) Inference always uses more memory because of KV cache; gradient accumulation is irrelevant.
questions.explanation: During training you typically need: parameters, gradients, optimizer state (often 2–4× parameter size for Adam-like optimizers), and saved activations for backprop (often the largest term, scaling with batch/seq). Inference usually doesn’t store backward activations, and can run with lower memory (though KV cache can matter for autoregressive decoding). Gradient accumulation helps when you want a large effective batch but need smaller per-step activations; it can hurt throughput due to extra forward/backward passes, and may change communication overlap patterns in distributed training.

questions.question_text:
Memory layout “spot the bug”: The following code gets much slower after a refactor. What happened and how do you fix it?

```python
# x: [B, T, H] on CUDA
x = x.transpose(0, 1)          # now [T, B, H]
x = x.view(-1, x.size(-1))     # flatten to [T*B, H]
```
questions.correct_answer:
Problem: `transpose` returns a non-contiguous view with different strides. Calling `.view(...)` requires contiguous memory; in recent PyTorch this will either error or (in some contexts) trigger an implicit contiguous copy via `.reshape`-like behavior if changed, causing extra memory traffic and slowdown.
Fix:
- Use `reshape` with awareness it may copy, or make it explicit:
  - `x = x.transpose(0, 1).contiguous().view(-1, x.size(-1))`
- Or avoid the transpose/flatten pattern by using `x = x.permute(...).reshape(...)` and ensure downstream ops accept non-contiguous tensors, or keep original layout.
- In performance-sensitive paths, minimize unnecessary transposes and prefer layouts expected by kernels.
questions.explanation: ML performance is often limited by memory movement. Non-contiguous tensors can force hidden copies when you request a different shape with `view` or when kernels require contiguous inputs. The fix is either to keep tensors contiguous in the expected layout or to make the copy explicit and amortize it (or avoid it entirely).

questions.question_text: You run `torch.profiler`/Nsight and see thousands of tiny elementwise ops (adds, muls, layernorm pieces) dominating CPU time and showing many short GPU kernels with gaps between them. What is the most likely bottleneck and best high-level fix?
options: A) Memory-bound; fix by increasing dataloader workers.
B) Kernel launch overhead / Python dispatch; fix by operator fusion (e.g., `torch.compile`, fused kernels like fused attention/MLP, or using higher-level fused ops).
C) Compute-bound; fix by switching from bf16 to fp32.
D) Communication-bound; fix by increasing DDP bucket size.
questions.explanation: Many tiny kernels with gaps often indicate launch-bound behavior: the overhead of launching kernels and Python/framework dispatch dominates. Fusion reduces the number of launches and improves locality (fewer reads/writes to global memory). In practice, `torch.compile`, using fused optimizers, fused layernorm, FlashAttention/SDPA, or rewriting code to use fewer pointwise ops are common fixes.

questions.question_text: Batch size / sequence length scaling: You increase batch size and see throughput (tokens/s or samples/s) improve at first, then saturate, and eventually iteration time increases a lot while GPU util becomes noisy. Which explanation is most accurate?
options: A) Larger batches always reduce kernel launch overhead, so throughput must monotonically increase until OOM.

B) Throughput improves while you amortize fixed overheads and increase math per launch, then saturates when you hit compute or memory bandwidth limits; very large batches can worsen due to memory pressure (activation/optimizer), poorer cache behavior, or reduced overlap, and can increase latency even if throughput is similar.
C) Saturation only happens because the dataloader becomes slower at higher batch sizes; GPU limits are irrelevant.
D) If throughput saturates, it means you need to disable mixed precision.
questions.explanation: Batch/sequence scaling typically shows: (1) better utilization and amortized overhead initially, (2) saturation at a hardware limit (compute-bound or memory-bound), and (3) degradation when memory pressure increases (more activations, larger attention matrices, more spills/fragmentation), or when kernels become less efficient. Also, larger batches often hurt latency (time-to-first-token / per-request latency) even if throughput is high. Microbatching/gradient accumulation can preserve global batch while keeping per-step memory manageable.

questions.question_text:
Spot the PyTorch GPU “footgun” in the code below and explain the performance impact. Provide a fix.

```python
losses = []
for batch in loader:
    x, y = batch
    x = x.cuda(non_blocking=True)
    y = y.cuda(non_blocking=True)
    out = model(x)
    loss = criterion(out, y)
    losses.append(loss.item())
```
questions.correct_answer:
Footgun: `loss.item()` forces a synchronous GPU→CPU transfer and a device synchronization because it needs the scalar value on CPU immediately. This can introduce a sync point every iteration, preventing overlap and reducing throughput.
Fixes:
- Accumulate losses on GPU and only transfer occasionally:
  - `losses.append(loss.detach())` and at the end `torch.stack(losses).mean().item()`.
- Or log less frequently:
  - `if step % k == 0: log(loss.detach().float().mean().cpu())`.
- If using distributed, reduce asynchronously or use `torchmetrics` patterns that avoid per-step sync.
questions.explanation: Many seemingly harmless CPU-side actions (like `.item()`, `.cpu()`, printing tensors) create implicit synchronization, turning an otherwise asynchronous pipeline into a step-by-step barrier. The fix is to reduce the frequency of device-to-host transfers and keep intermediate values on GPU until needed.

questions.question_text: In PyTorch, when can H2D (CPU→GPU) copies overlap with GPU compute in a typical training loop, and what pattern enables it?
options: A) Always; all `.to('cuda')` calls overlap automatically regardless of memory type and stream usage.

B) Only when the source CPU tensors are in pinned (page-locked) memory and the copy is enqueued asynchronously (e.g., `non_blocking=True`) so it can run on the copy engine while compute runs on another stream.
C) Only on NVLink systems; PCIe can never overlap.
D) Only if you call `torch.cuda.synchronize()` after the copy to “start it earlier”.
questions.explanation: For overlap you need (1) pinned host memory (so DMA can proceed without paging), (2) an async copy (`non_blocking=True` when moving from pinned CPU tensors), and (3) a scheduling pattern that enqueues the copy ahead of compute (often via prefetching the next batch). Overlap can happen on PCIe too, depending on hardware copy engines and stream usage. Calling `synchronize()` forces a wait and reduces overlap.

questions.question_text: You’re asked to do a quick GPU performance triage for a training job that “only uses 30–40% GPU”. Which checklist best matches a practical ML engineer workflow to determine whether you’re compute-bound, memory-bound, launch-bound, or input-bound?
options: A) Only look at `nvidia-smi` utilization; if it’s low, it’s always input-bound.

B) Start with GPU util and iteration time; then check dataloader time, H2D copy time, kernel time breakdown (profiler), and memory throughput vs SM activity; map each bottleneck to actions (increase batch, fuse ops, pin memory, prefetch, use AMP, etc.).
C) Immediately rewrite kernels in CUDA; profiling is optional.
D) Increase batch size until OOM; if it OOMs, it was compute-bound.
questions.explanation:
A practical triage starts coarse (iteration time, GPU util) and then uses targeted measurements to classify the bottleneck:
- Input-bound: dataloader/CPU preprocessing dominates; GPU idle gaps; fix with more workers, caching, vectorized transforms, pinned memory, prefetch.
- H2D/copy-bound: large memcpy time; fix with pinned memory, `non_blocking=True`, better batching, avoiding extra copies.
- Launch-bound: many tiny kernels/ops; low SM occupancy; fix with operator fusion, larger batch, `torch.compile`, fused attention/MLP.
- Memory-bound: high memory throughput, low compute; fix with better layout, fusion, reduced precision, kernel choices.
- Compute-bound: high SM activity; fix by using tensor cores (AMP), better kernels, scaling across GPUs.
This is the typical workflow for ML engineers.

questions.question_text: You suspect a GPU OOM happens only at a specific step (e.g., first forward after enabling gradient checkpointing, or after a certain batch). Give a practical playbook to (1) reliably reproduce the peak, (2) measure peak allocated vs reserved, and (3) apply 3–5 common fixes. Mention at least one allocator-related mitigation.
questions.correct_answer:
Playbook example:
1) Reproduce peak deterministically:
- Fix seeds and data order (disable shuffling or log/replay the exact batch indices).
- Run the exact code path that triggers peak (e.g., first iteration includes compilation/cudnn autotune; warm up separately if needed).
- Use `torch.cuda.synchronize()` around suspected regions to attribute peaks to phases.
- Use `torch.cuda.reset_peak_memory_stats()` right before the region and then read `max_memory_allocated()` / `max_memory_reserved()` after.
2) Measure/attribute:
- Record `torch.cuda.memory_allocated()`, `torch.cuda.memory_reserved()`, and peaks (`max_memory_allocated`, `max_memory_reserved`) per step.
- Use `torch.cuda.memory_summary()` to inspect inactive split blocks (fragmentation) and large allocations.
- Optionally profile with Nsight Systems/torch.profiler to find the op that triggers the peak.
3) Common fixes:
- Reduce batch size and/or sequence length (often the largest lever for activations/attention).
- Use gradient accumulation (microbatching) to keep global batch while reducing per-step activation memory.
- Enable activation checkpointing (trade compute for memory; ensure it’s applied to the right blocks).
- Use mixed precision (fp16/bf16) and/or gradient scaling.
- Offload: optimizer state offload (e.g., ZeRO, CPU offload), activation offload, or use parameter sharding.
- Use fused/efficient attention kernels (FlashAttention / SDPA) to reduce activation memory.
Allocator-related:
- Set `PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=max_split_size_mb:<N>` to reduce fragmentation; consider `garbage_collection_threshold`.
- Avoid many varying-size allocations; reuse buffers where possible.
- As a last resort, call `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` between phases (doesn’t reduce allocated tensors, but can drop reserved blocks back to CUDA).
questions.explanation: A good OOM workflow separates (a) true tensor peak (allocated) from (b) allocator reservation/fragmentation (reserved). Reproduction matters because peaks can be data-dependent (sequence length), path-dependent (first step autotune/compile), or phase-dependent (optimizer step). Fixes map to the dominant contributor: activations (batch/seq, checkpointing, efficient attention), optimizer state (ZeRO/offload), or fragmentation (allocator settings, reducing allocation churn).

questions.question_text:
You hit a CUDA OOM in PyTorch. `torch.cuda.memory_summary()` shows something like:
- allocated: 10.2 GiB
- reserved: 23.5 GiB
- free (from CUDA): small
but your model + batch “should” only need ~11–12 GiB. What is the most likely interpretation and the best next step to confirm?
options: A) True memory leak in model parameters; confirm by checking `model.parameters()` size and restarting fixes it permanently.
B) CUDNN workspace is permanently pinned; confirm by disabling cuDNN.

C) Caching allocator reservation/fragmentation: reserved >> allocated; confirm by reproducing with identical peak and inspecting peak stats (`max_memory_allocated`, `max_memory_reserved`) and allocator behavior.
D) PCIe bandwidth issue; confirm by running `nvidia-smi dmon` and looking for low RX/TX.
questions.explanation: In PyTorch, the CUDA caching allocator often reserves more memory than is currently allocated to tensors. If reserved is much larger than allocated, you may be seeing allocator fragmentation or a previous peak that caused the allocator to grab (reserve) large blocks that it keeps for reuse. A good next step is to reproduce the same peak (same batch/seq, same graph path) and compare `torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated()` and `torch.cuda.max_memory_reserved()`; also use `torch.cuda.memory_summary()` after the failing step to see inactive split blocks. This distinguishes “true need” (allocated peak) from allocator behavior (reserved/fragmentation).

questions.question_text: In LLM inference serving, why do throughput/latency characteristics differ between the prefill phase and the decode phase, and what is one GPU optimization beyond simple batching that targets decode efficiency?
questions.correct_answer: Prefill processes the full prompt in parallel, so it has large matrix multiplies and better GPU utilization; decode generates tokens step-by-step, often with small per-step compute and growing KV cache, making it more memory/bandwidth bound and sensitive to kernel launch overhead. An optimization beyond batching is continuous batching (dynamically forming batches each decode step) and/or using paged attention (managing KV cache in blocks to reduce fragmentation and improve memory locality), which helps keep the GPU busy while controlling KV cache growth and latency.
questions.explanation: Prefill resembles standard dense training/inference: big GEMMs with high arithmetic intensity. Decode is iterative and dominated by attention over cached keys/values plus smaller GEMMs, so scheduling and memory management matter more. Techniques like continuous batching, speculative decoding, and paged KV cache aim to improve utilization and reduce tail latency under multi-tenant workloads.

questions.question_text:
You are measuring GPU time for a forward pass and write:

```python
start = time.time()
out = model(x)
end = time.time()
print(end - start)
```

Why can this be misleading on GPU, and what is the correct way to time it in PyTorch?
questions.correct_answer: GPU ops are asynchronous: `model(x)` enqueues work and returns before the GPU finishes, so `time.time()` measures mostly CPU enqueue time. Correct timing uses synchronization or CUDA events, e.g. `torch.cuda.synchronize()` before/after, or better: create `torch.cuda.Event(enable_timing=True)` start/end, record them around the region, then `torch.cuda.synchronize()` and read `start.elapsed_time(end)` for accurate GPU milliseconds.
questions.explanation: Asynchrony is a feature: it allows CPU and GPU to overlap. But it breaks naive wall-clock timing. CUDA events measure time on the GPU timeline and avoid over-synchronizing. Also watch for accidental syncs (e.g., `.item()`, `.cpu()`, printing tensors) that can distort measurements and performance.

questions.question_text: You profile a model and see thousands of tiny elementwise ops (adds, muls, reshapes) around layernorm/activation, and GPU utilization is low despite high theoretical FLOPs. Explain why this happens and name two practical fixes an AI/ML engineer might try.
questions.correct_answer: Many tiny ops lead to high kernel launch overhead and poor memory efficiency: each op launches a separate kernel, does little work, and often reads/writes intermediates to global memory. This prevents the GPU from staying busy. Practical fixes: (1) enable/try operator fusion via `torch.compile`, fused kernels (e.g., fused layernorm, fused MLP), or using libraries like Apex/xFormers; (2) increase arithmetic intensity by increasing batch size/sequence length where feasible, or restructure code to reduce intermediate tensors (in-place where safe). Also reduce Python-side overhead by avoiding per-token loops and using vectorized ops.
questions.explanation: Modern GPUs need large, well-structured kernels to amortize launch overhead and maximize memory coalescing and cache reuse. Elementwise chains often become bandwidth-bound and suffer from extra reads/writes. Fusion combines multiple ops into one kernel, reducing launches and intermediate traffic. Increasing batch/sequence can also make each kernel do more work, improving occupancy—though it can increase memory usage.

questions.question_text: You want to maximize Tensor Core utilization for GEMMs in a Transformer. Which configuration is most likely to hit fast paths on modern NVIDIA GPUs?
options: A. Use fp32 everywhere and choose hidden sizes that are prime numbers to avoid alignment artifacts
B. Use bf16/fp16 (or TF32 where applicable) and choose matmul dimensions that are multiples of 8 or 16 (hardware-dependent), avoiding odd head dimensions
C. Use int64 weights to reduce rounding error, and any shape will use Tensor Cores
D. Use fp16 but ensure batch size is 1 to reduce memory pressure
questions.explanation: Tensor Cores accelerate matrix operations for certain dtypes (fp16/bf16, and TF32 on Ampere+ for fp32 inputs) and prefer aligned dimensions (often multiples of 8/16). In real models, this shows up as choosing head dimensions and hidden sizes that align well (e.g., 64/128) and ensuring the kernel can use the intended fast path. Misaligned shapes can fall back to slower kernels or achieve lower occupancy/efficiency.

questions.question_text: You notice `torch.cuda.memory_reserved()` is high and grows over time, but `torch.cuda.memory_allocated()` is relatively stable, and you hit OOM on a later allocation. What is the most likely explanation in PyTorch?
options: A. CUDA kernels are leaking registers into global memory
B. The caching allocator reserves blocks and can suffer from fragmentation, so reserved memory stays high and large contiguous blocks may be unavailable
C. `memory_allocated()` includes CPU RAM, not VRAM
D. `memory_reserved()` only counts model parameters, not activations
questions.explanation: PyTorch uses a caching allocator: it reserves GPU memory in blocks to speed up future allocations. Over time, allocation patterns can fragment the reserved pool, making it hard to satisfy a large allocation even if total allocated bytes look fine. Practical mitigations include reducing allocation churn (fewer shape changes), using `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` sparingly (it returns cached blocks to CUDA but doesn’t fix all issues), bucketing shapes, or setting allocator configuration (e.g., max split size) depending on the workload.

questions.question_text: A model fits in VRAM for forward-only inference but OOMs during training. Explain what additional GPU memory consumers appear in training and give one practical way to reduce peak memory without changing model architecture.
questions.correct_answer: Training adds (a) gradients for parameters, (b) optimizer state (e.g., Adam keeps momentum and variance buffers—often ~2x parameter size), and (c) saved activations needed for backward (can be large, especially with long sequences). A practical way to reduce peak memory is activation checkpointing (recompute activations during backward), or using a memory-efficient optimizer (e.g., AdamW with fused/8-bit states), reducing batch/sequence length, or using mixed precision (bf16/fp16) to cut activation/gradient memory.
questions.explanation: Inference mainly needs parameters + intermediate activations for the current forward. Training must retain information to compute gradients and often stores multiple extra buffers. Understanding this breakdown helps diagnose why a model that “fits” for inference can still OOM in training. Techniques like checkpointing trade compute for memory and are commonly used in large-model training.

questions.question_text: You have variable-length sequences and observe low GPU utilization due to padding. Which dataset-side strategy most directly reduces wasted attention compute while keeping implementation simple?
options: A. Randomly shuffle sequences each epoch with no length awareness
B. Bucket examples by similar lengths (or sort within a window) and batch within buckets to minimize padding
C. Always pad every sequence to the maximum length in the entire dataset
D. Convert all sequences to Python lists to avoid tensor padding
questions.explanation: Attention compute (and memory) typically scales with sequence length (often O(T^2) for standard attention). Excess padding makes T larger than necessary, wasting FLOPs and VRAM. Bucketing by length is a common, low-friction approach: it reduces padding while preserving vectorized batching. More advanced approaches include packing, ragged tensors, or specialized kernels, but bucketing is often the first practical win.

questions.question_text: You’re training a Transformer and see occasional NaNs in attention/softmax under fp16. Describe two practical changes an AI/ML engineer might make to improve numerical stability without reverting the entire model to fp32.
questions.correct_answer: Two common fixes are: (1) compute softmax (and sometimes attention score accumulation) in higher precision (fp32 accumulation) while keeping inputs/outputs in fp16/bf16, e.g., cast logits to fp32 for softmax then cast back; (2) use scaling and stable formulations: subtract max before softmax (often already done), apply attention scaling (1/sqrt(d)), use fused attention kernels (e.g., FlashAttention) that implement numerically stable softmax and better accumulation. Additional practical knobs include using bf16 instead of fp16, enabling GradScaler (for fp16), and clamping logits or using dropout carefully.
questions.explanation: Softmax is sensitive because exponentials amplify magnitude; fp16’s limited exponent range can overflow, and limited mantissa can lose small differences. Many production kernels keep the bulk of compute in fp16/bf16 but do reductions/exp/sum in fp32 to stabilize. Fused attention implementations also reduce intermediate materialization and use stable reductions, improving both stability and performance.

questions.question_text: In mixed-precision training on recent NVIDIA GPUs, when is `bfloat16` typically preferred over `float16`?
options: A. When you need more exponent range to reduce overflow/underflow risk while still getting Tensor Core speedups
B. When you need exact integer arithmetic for embedding lookups
C. When you want smaller memory than fp16
D. When you want higher mantissa precision than fp32
questions.explanation: `bf16` has the same 8-bit exponent as fp32 (larger dynamic range) but fewer mantissa bits than fp16. This makes it more stable for training (less overflow in activations/gradients) while still enabling fast Tensor Core paths on supported hardware. It does not use less memory than fp16 (both are 16-bit) and it does not have more precision than fp32.

questions.question_text: A team adopts `torch.compile(model)` and sees speedups on fixed-shape batches, but performance collapses when sequence lengths vary per batch. What is the most likely reason?
options: A. `torch.compile` disables Tensor Cores automatically when sequence lengths vary
B. Variable shapes can trigger frequent graph breaks or recompilations, adding overhead and preventing stable optimized graphs
C. Variable shapes always cause CUDA OOM due to larger temporary buffers
D. `torch.compile` forces all tensors to be contiguous, increasing copies
questions.explanation: Modern compilers/graph capture rely on stable shapes and control flow to build reusable optimized graphs. If sequence length changes often, the compiler may recompile many variants or break the graph, adding Python overhead and preventing kernel fusion. Mitigations include bucketing by length, padding to a small set of sizes, using dynamic-shape support where available, or constraining shapes in the input pipeline.

questions.question_text: Which PyTorch snippet is most likely to introduce a GPU synchronization point and reduce throughput in a training loop?
options: A. `loss = loss_fn(out, y); loss.backward()`
B. `running_loss += loss.item()`
C. `out = model(x)`
D. `optimizer.step()`
questions.explanation: Calling `.item()` transfers a scalar from GPU to CPU and forces the host to wait for all preceding GPU work to finish (a sync). Doing this every iteration can severely reduce overlap and throughput. The other operations are typically enqueued asynchronously on the GPU (though they may include internal syncs in some cases). A common mitigation is to log less frequently, accumulate on GPU, or use CUDA events for timing instead of host reads.

questions.question_text:
You see a model slow down after adding a transpose before a linear layer:

```python
x = x.transpose(1, 2)          # [B, T, C] -> [B, C, T]
y = torch.nn.functional.linear(x, W)  # W: [out, in]
```

In PyTorch, when does calling `x = x.contiguous()` most likely improve GPU performance, and why?
questions.correct_answer: `x.contiguous()` helps when `x` is a non-contiguous view (e.g., from transpose/permute) and the subsequent ops (like GEMM/linear, conv, or fused kernels) either require contiguous memory or perform much better with contiguous/expected stride layouts. Making it contiguous triggers a copy into a contiguous buffer so the following kernel can use coalesced memory accesses and/or fast-path implementations. It can hurt if it adds an extra copy that wasn’t needed (some ops handle strided inputs efficiently), so you use it when profiling shows the downstream op is bottlenecked by the strided layout or falling back to a slower kernel.
questions.explanation: Many tensor transformations in PyTorch (transpose/permute/slice) return *views* with different strides, not copies. A non-contiguous tensor can force kernels to do strided loads (worse memory coalescing) or select a slower implementation. `contiguous()` materializes a compact layout (a copy) so the next kernel can read linearly and hit optimized paths. The tradeoff is that the copy itself costs time and memory bandwidth, so it should be guided by profiling and knowledge of which ops are sensitive to layout.

questions.question_text: Transformer attention has well-known GPU implications. Explain how sequence length affects compute and memory for attention during training, and describe (at a high level) what FlashAttention/memory-efficient attention changes. Include why this can reduce OOM risk and/or improve speed.
questions.correct_answer:
Expected points:
- Standard full attention forms an attention matrix of size (seq_len × seq_len) per head (and per batch). Compute scales roughly O(B * H * S^2 * d_head) and memory for attention weights/softmax intermediates scales O(B * H * S^2). This quadratic scaling makes long sequences expensive and often memory-limited.
- During training, additional memory is needed to store intermediates for backward (softmax outputs, dropout masks, etc.), increasing activation memory and OOM risk at large S.
- FlashAttention/memory-efficient attention avoids materializing the full S×S attention matrix in GPU memory by tiling/blocking, fusing operations (QK^T, softmax, dropout, V projection) and recomputing or streaming partial results. This reduces memory traffic and peak memory, often turning a memory-bound kernel into a more compute-efficient one.
- Practical outcome: lower peak VRAM (enables longer sequences or larger batch), higher throughput due to fewer reads/writes and better kernel fusion; tradeoffs can include constraints on dtype/hardware and different numerical behavior (usually acceptable).
questions.explanation: Attention’s quadratic scaling is often the first thing that breaks when increasing context length. Memory-efficient attention techniques primarily reduce peak activation/workspace memory and memory bandwidth, which are common bottlenecks on GPUs for transformers.

questions.question_text:
Your dataloader uses pinned memory, but the GPU still shows periodic idle gaps. Which set of changes is most likely to improve the input pipeline throughput and reduce stalls in a typical image/text training job?

Assume you use PyTorch `DataLoader` and the dataset does CPU decode/augment.
options: A. Increase `num_workers`, enable `persistent_workers=True`, tune `prefetch_factor`, and profile CPU decode/augment; consider caching or moving heavy augmentations off the main thread
B. Disable pinned memory because it can slow down transfers
C. Call `torch.cuda.synchronize()` each iteration to make timing accurate and reduce stalls
D. Set `shuffle=False` so the GPU can predict the next batch
questions.explanation: Pinned memory helps H2D transfer speed and enables overlap, but it doesn’t fix CPU-side bottlenecks like decode/augment, Python overhead, or insufficient prefetching. Increasing workers, keeping workers alive (persistent), tuning prefetch, and profiling CPU stages address the common root causes of GPU idle gaps. Adding synchronizations typically makes things worse; disabling shuffle rarely addresses throughput.

questions.question_text: You are asked to reason about end-to-end training performance. Given a training job that is slow, outline how you would determine whether the bottleneck is (a) GPU compute, (b) GPU memory bandwidth / memory-bound kernels, (c) CPU/input pipeline, or (d) distributed communication. Include at least one concrete measurement/tool or symptom for each and the “lever” you would try first.
questions.correct_answer:
A good answer includes a structured approach:

(a) GPU compute-bound
- Measure: high sustained SM utilization; kernels dominated by GEMMs/conv; Nsight Systems/Compute or PyTorch profiler shows large matmul time.
- Symptom: speed improves with faster GPU or higher clocks; increasing batch size improves throughput until memory limits.
- Lever: increase arithmetic intensity (larger batch/microbatch), fuse ops (fused optimizers), use TF32/bf16/fp16, ensure cuDNN/cuBLAS tuned, use `torch.compile` where appropriate.

(b) GPU memory-bandwidth / memory-bound
- Measure: low SM utilization but high memory throughput; profiler shows many elementwise ops, layernorm, softmax, attention memory traffic.
- Symptom: increasing batch doesn’t help much; kernels have low occupancy/low FLOPs/byte.
- Lever: kernel fusion (fused LN/MLP), FlashAttention/memory-efficient attention, reduce activation size (seq/batch), use better layouts, avoid redundant casts/copies.

(c) CPU/input pipeline-bound
- Measure: GPU utilization has gaps; dataloader time high; `torch.profiler` shows `DataLoader`/CPU ops dominating; CPU cores pegged; disk/network IO waits.
- Lever: increase `num_workers`, enable `persistent_workers`, tune `prefetch_factor`, move decode/augment to GPU or faster libs, cache/preprocess, use pinned memory + non_blocking H2D, avoid per-iter sync (e.g., `.item()` spam).

(d) Distributed communication-bound
- Measure: NCCL/all-reduce time high; scaling efficiency drops with more GPUs; poor overlap of comm with backward.
- Lever: increase per-GPU batch (reduce comm/compute ratio), gradient bucketing tuning, overlap improvements, reduce gradient size (fp16/bf16 grads), use ZeRO/FSDP sharding, optimize network/topology.

The best answers tie each bottleneck to an observable metric and a first-line mitigation.
questions.explanation: End-to-end performance is a balance of compute, memory, input, and communication. The key skill is to identify the dominant term with measurements and then choose the lever that reduces that term (not a generic ‘optimize everything’).

questions.question_text:
You scale from 1 GPU to 8 GPUs with PyTorch DDP and see only ~3.5x speedup. Which observation most strongly suggests gradient communication (all-reduce) is the bottleneck rather than dataloader or pure compute?

Assume you already verified the input pipeline is not obviously stuck.
options: A. GPU utilization is high, but step time increases significantly when enabling DDP even with the same global batch size; profiler shows time in NCCL/all-reduce and poor overlap with backward
B. GPU utilization is low and CPU usage is pegged at 100% in `top`
C. VRAM usage is close to full and occasional OOM happens
D. Loss becomes NaN after scaling to 8 GPUs
questions.explanation: DDP adds gradient synchronization (all-reduce). If the profiler attributes a large fraction of step time to NCCL/all-reduce and it doesn’t overlap well with backward compute, scaling efficiency drops. Low GPU utilization with high CPU usage points more to input pipeline/CPU bottlenecks; OOM is memory; NaNs are numerical/stability issues.

questions.question_text: You want to train a model that no longer fits on a single GPU, and later scale to multiple nodes. At a conceptual level, when would you choose data parallelism vs model parallelism (tensor) vs pipeline parallelism? Pick the best statement.
options: A. Data parallel when the model fits per GPU; model/tensor parallel when parameters/activations don’t fit; pipeline parallel when layers can be split into stages but you can tolerate pipeline bubbles and want to scale very large models
B. Model parallel is always better than data parallel because it avoids gradient synchronization
C. Pipeline parallel is only useful on a single node; it cannot work across nodes
D. Data parallel is only for inference; training requires model parallel
questions.explanation: Data parallel replicates the model on each GPU and synchronizes gradients; it’s simplest and efficient when the model fits in one GPU’s memory. Tensor/model parallel splits individual layers/tensors across GPUs to fit larger models but adds communication in the forward/backward. Pipeline parallel splits the model by layer blocks into stages; it can scale to very large models but introduces pipeline bubbles and scheduling complexity. In practice, large-scale training often combines these (e.g., DP × TP × PP).

questions.question_text: You are optimizing inference for an LLM service. Compare training vs inference GPU considerations in terms of throughput vs latency and memory. Your answer must mention batching, latency constraints, and KV-cache memory at a high level, and explain why optimal settings differ between training and inference.
questions.correct_answer:
Key points:
- Training optimizes tokens/sec and typically uses large batches/sequence lengths; it must store activations for backward, plus gradients and optimizer state. Memory is dominated by activations (and optimizer state for large models).
- Inference often has latency constraints (time-to-first-token, per-token latency) and variable request sizes. Large batches improve throughput but can hurt latency; dynamic batching trades latency for higher GPU utilization.
- Autoregressive decoding uses a KV cache: for each layer, keys/values for past tokens are stored to avoid recomputing attention. KV cache memory scales with batch_size * sequence_length * num_layers * hidden_size (times bytes per element). This can dominate inference memory even though there are no gradients/optimizer states.
- Optimal settings differ: training can accept higher latency per step and aims for stable large batches; inference must balance batch size and scheduling for SLA, and may prefer smaller micro-batches, quantization, paged KV cache, or tensor parallelism depending on latency/throughput targets.
- In inference you often use `torch.no_grad()`/`inference_mode()` and possibly lower precision/quantization; in training you use AMP but keep some states in fp32 for stability.
questions.explanation: The core difference is what must be stored and what metric matters. Training stores backward information and cares about steady-state throughput; inference stores KV cache for fast decoding and often cares about tail latency. These differences drive different batching, precision, and memory strategies.

questions.question_text:
Consider the code below:

```python
# inside training loop
for batch in loader:
    x = batch['x']          # CPU tensor
    x = x.to('cuda')
    x = x.float()
    y = model(x)
```

Which change best minimizes unnecessary data movement/copies and avoids repeated casts, assuming the model expects `float16` on GPU and you use AMP?
options: A. Move and cast once: `x = batch['x'].to(device='cuda', dtype=torch.float16, non_blocking=True)` (and ensure loader uses pinned memory); avoid extra `.float()`
B. Keep `x` on CPU and set `model = model.cpu()` to avoid transfers
C. Call `x = x.cuda()` twice to make sure it is on GPU before forward
D. Convert to NumPy first (`x.numpy()`) and then back to torch on GPU to ensure contiguous memory
questions.explanation: Every `.to(...)`/dtype conversion can trigger a new allocation and copy. Combining device + dtype in one `.to(...)` reduces extra copies. With pinned host memory and `non_blocking=True`, H2D transfers can overlap with compute. Under AMP, you often keep inputs in fp16/bf16 (as appropriate) and let autocast handle internal casting; an explicit `.float()` is both unnecessary and can increase bandwidth and memory.

questions.question_text: You hit CUDA OOM during training a transformer. Give a practical, ordered playbook to (1) identify what is consuming VRAM and (2) mitigate it. Your answer should explicitly mention at least: activations, gradients, optimizer state, and 4+ concrete mitigation techniques (e.g., checkpointing, accumulation, offload, mixed precision, smaller seq/batch).
questions.correct_answer:
A strong answer should include: 

1) Identify consumers
- Recognize main VRAM buckets: model parameters, activations (saved for backward), gradients, optimizer states (e.g., Adam has momentum/variance ~2x params), temporary buffers (attention, layernorm, matmul workspaces), and cached allocator fragmentation.
- Use tools: `torch.cuda.memory_summary()`, `torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated()`, `torch.cuda.memory_allocated()/reserved()`, PyTorch profiler memory view, and step-wise measurement around forward/backward/step.
- Compare training vs eval: in eval (`torch.no_grad()`), activations aren’t stored, so memory drops.
- Check sequence length and batch size effects (activations/attention scale strongly with them).

2) Mitigate (typical order)
- Reduce batch size and/or sequence length (fastest lever; often activation-dominated).
- Mixed precision (AMP/bfloat16/fp16) to reduce activation/grad/optimizer memory; consider keeping some states in fp32.
- Gradient accumulation to keep effective batch size while lowering per-step activation memory (trade: more steps, more time).
- Activation/gradient checkpointing to recompute activations during backward (trade: extra compute).
- Use memory-efficient attention / FlashAttention to reduce attention activation and workspace.
- Optimizer changes: switch Adam -> AdamW with fused implementations, 8-bit optimizers, or reduce optimizer state (trade: convergence/quality considerations).
- Offload: optimizer state/parameters to CPU (ZeRO-Offload) or NVMe (trade: PCIe latency).
- Distributed strategies: ZeRO/FSDP to shard parameters/gradients/optimizer states across GPUs.
- Ensure `optimizer.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)` and avoid retaining graphs (`retain_graph=True` only if needed).
- Fix leaks: don’t store tensors from forward in Python lists without `.detach()`; avoid holding onto computation graphs.

A good answer is explicit about which technique targets which bucket (activations vs optimizer state vs params) and notes tradeoffs.
questions.explanation: OOM debugging is about mapping memory to buckets and then choosing the lever that reduces the dominant bucket. For many transformer trainings, activations and attention buffers dominate; for very large models, optimizer state and parameters can dominate. The best candidates connect mitigations to root cause and mention tradeoffs (compute vs memory, throughput vs latency, convergence).

questions.question_text:
In a PyTorch training loop, which of the following operations is most likely to introduce an implicit GPU synchronization (making the CPU wait for the GPU), potentially hurting throughput?

Assume `loss` is a CUDA tensor scalar and `pred` is a CUDA tensor.
options: A. `loss.item()` used every iteration for logging
B. `loss.backward()`
C. `optimizer.step()`
D. `pred = pred + 1`
questions.explanation: Calling `.item()` transfers a scalar value from GPU to CPU, which requires the GPU to finish all prior work that produces that value—an implicit synchronization point. Backward/step do GPU work but do not inherently force CPU-GPU sync unless you read results on the host. Simple tensor ops like `pred + 1` are also typically asynchronous launches.

questions.question_text:
You run the following PyTorch code and see surprisingly low GPU utilization in `nvidia-smi` while the script is running:

```python
import torch, time
x = torch.randn(4096, 4096, device='cuda')
W = torch.randn(4096, 4096, device='cuda')

start = time.time()
for _ in range(200):
    y = x @ W
end = time.time()
print('elapsed', end - start)
```

What is the most likely reason the timing is misleading, and what is the simplest fix to get an accurate elapsed time for GPU work?
options: A. GPU kernels execute asynchronously; use `torch.cuda.synchronize()` (or CUDA events) before reading the end time
B. PyTorch uses CPU fallback for `@`; set `torch.set_default_device('cuda')` to force GPU
C. `nvidia-smi` cannot show utilization for matrix multiply; use `torch.profiler` only
D. The GPU is underclocked; set persistence mode to true to get correct timing
questions.explanation: Most CUDA launches in PyTorch are asynchronous with respect to the host: the loop enqueues work quickly, and `time.time()` measures mostly enqueue time, not execution time. Insert `torch.cuda.synchronize()` before stopping the timer (or use CUDA events) to ensure all queued kernels finish before reading the end timestamp. Low/erratic utilization can also happen if the workload is too small or there are sync points elsewhere, but the immediate issue here is host-side timing without synchronization.

questions.question_text: You have variable-length sequences and currently pad all samples to the maximum length in the batch. Which approach most directly reduces wasted GPU compute from padding while keeping training semantics correct?
options: A. Always pad to a global maximum length for the entire dataset to make shapes static.
B. Use sequence packing/bucketing (group similar lengths) so batches have less padding, and/or pack multiple sequences into one long sequence with proper attention masks.
C. Convert the model to FP64 so padded tokens are cheaper to compute.
D. Disable attention masks so the model ignores padding automatically.
questions.explanation: Padding increases token count processed, which increases both compute and memory traffic (especially in attention). Bucketing by length reduces padding within each batch; packing can further increase utilization by concatenating sequences and using masks/segment IDs to preserve correctness. These techniques often yield meaningful speedups without changing the model’s mathematical intent.

questions.question_text: In transformer training, you keep batch size fixed but increase sequence length (e.g., 512 → 2048). Training slows down dramatically and may OOM. Give two concrete reasons (one compute-related and one memory-related) why this happens.
questions.correct_answer: Compute-related: self-attention has O(L^2) operations in sequence length L (attention score and weighted sum), so quadrupling L can increase attention compute by ~16×. Memory-related: attention often materializes or streams large intermediate tensors (e.g., Q/K/V, attention logits/probabilities, activations for backprop), many of which scale as O(L^2) or O(L·d); longer sequences increase activation memory and can trigger OOM, especially during backprop when storing activations.
questions.explanation: Sequence length changes the shape in a way that affects both arithmetic complexity and activation footprint. This is why techniques like FlashAttention, activation checkpointing, and sequence packing are common: they reduce memory traffic/materialization and improve locality while addressing L^2 scaling pain points.

questions.question_text: You increase batch size from 16 to 128 and see GPU utilization improve and step time per batch increase less than 8× (good scaling). Which explanation best matches what’s happening?
options: A. Larger batches always reduce total FLOPs, so training becomes cheaper.
B. Larger batches can increase kernel sizes/parallelism and amortize fixed overheads, improving throughput until you hit memory limits or different bottlenecks.
C. Larger batches make the GPU run at a higher clock because it detects more work.
D. Larger batches improve accuracy, which makes computation faster.
questions.explanation: Batch size is a key performance lever: many GPU kernels become more efficient with larger matrices/tensors because there’s more parallel work, better occupancy, and less overhead per example (launch overhead, input pipeline overhead, etc.). Eventually performance plateaus or degrades due to VRAM limits, worse memory locality, or hitting a bandwidth/compute ceiling.

questions.question_text: Explain dynamic loss scaling in mixed-precision training: what problem does it solve, and how does it work at a high level?
questions.correct_answer: Loss scaling addresses gradient underflow when using low-precision (especially FP16) where small gradient values may round to zero. The idea is to multiply the loss by a scale factor before backprop, which scales up gradients into a representable range; then, before the optimizer step, gradients are unscaled back down. Dynamic loss scaling adjusts the scale automatically: if overflow/NaNs are detected, it reduces the scale; if training is stable for a while, it may increase the scale to maximize precision without overflow.
questions.explanation: FP16 has limited exponent range compared to FP32, so gradients can underflow or overflow more easily. Scaling shifts values into a safer range during backprop. Dynamic schemes make this robust without manual tuning and are commonly implemented in AMP frameworks.

questions.question_text: You switch training from FP32 to mixed precision and observe a large speedup on an NVIDIA GPU with tensor cores. Which statement is most accurate?
options: A. Mixed precision is faster mainly because FP16 uses fewer CPU cycles in Python.
B. Mixed precision can be faster because tensor cores accelerate matrix multiplications at lower precision, and reduced memory traffic can also help.
C. Mixed precision is always slower because it adds extra casting operations.
D. Mixed precision only helps inference, not training.
questions.explanation: Tensor cores provide very high throughput for certain matrix/tensor operations at reduced precision (FP16/BF16/TF32 depending on GPU and settings). Additionally, smaller data types reduce memory bandwidth pressure. There is overhead from casts and maintaining FP32 master weights in many setups, but the net effect is often a speedup for GEMM/conv/attention-heavy models.

questions.question_text: In a multi-GPU server, you see much faster GPU↔GPU communication on some GPU pairs than others. What hardware factor most commonly explains this difference for deep learning training?
options: A. Some GPUs have more VRAM, which directly increases inter-GPU bandwidth.
B. The PCIe generation alone fully determines GPU↔GPU bandwidth; topology never matters.
C. NVLink/NVSwitch connectivity and PCIe topology (same root complex vs across CPU sockets) affect peer-to-peer bandwidth/latency.
D. CUDA version is the main determinant of GPU↔GPU bandwidth.
questions.explanation: Inter-GPU performance depends heavily on the connectivity: NVLink/NVSwitch can provide much higher bandwidth than PCIe, and even within PCIe, topology matters (e.g., GPUs behind the same PCIe switch vs crossing CPU sockets/NUMA boundaries). This directly impacts DDP/all-reduce, pipeline parallelism, and activation/parameter sharding performance.

questions.question_text: You are training in PyTorch and see frequent Host→Device copies in the profiler. Which change most directly reduces input pipeline transfer overhead for typical DataLoader-based training?
options: A. Set DataLoader(num_workers=0) to avoid multiprocessing overhead.
B. Use pinned (page-locked) host memory and asynchronous copies (e.g., DataLoader(pin_memory=True) and non_blocking=True on .to(device)).
C. Convert all tensors to float64 so fewer conversions happen on GPU.
D. Call torch.cuda.synchronize() after every batch to keep transfers consistent.
questions.explanation: Pinned (page-locked) host memory enables faster and more predictable DMA transfers to the GPU and allows async H→D copies that can overlap with GPU compute when used correctly. In PyTorch, pin_memory=True plus non_blocking=True commonly reduces input stalls. Synchronizing every batch typically hurts overlap and throughput.

questions.question_text:
Conceptually, what is GPU “shared memory” (or “scratchpad” memory) and how does an ML kernel benefit from it?

Answer in 3–6 sentences; no need for CUDA code.
questions.correct_answer: Shared memory is a small, fast on-chip memory region shared by threads within a block (or threadgroup). It’s explicitly managed by the kernel and is much lower latency/higher bandwidth than VRAM (HBM/GDDR). ML kernels use it to stage tiles of activations/weights so multiple threads can reuse the same data without repeatedly fetching from global memory. This increases data reuse and arithmetic intensity, often turning a memory-bound operation into a more compute-efficient one (e.g., tiled GEMM/conv, attention tiling).
questions.explanation: The key idea is locality and reuse: global memory is large but relatively slow; shared memory is tiny but fast. By loading a tile once and reusing it across many multiply-adds, you reduce global memory traffic and improve throughput. Many high-performance tensor kernels are essentially careful tiling + shared-memory reuse + register blocking.

questions.question_text: You notice a model is slower on GPU than expected. Nsight/Profiler shows low SM (compute) utilization but very high memory throughput utilization (close to peak). Which interpretation is most likely?
options: A. The workload is compute-bound; increasing FLOPs will make it faster.
B. The workload is memory-bandwidth-bound; optimizing memory access patterns or reducing memory traffic may help more than adding compute.
C. The workload is PCIe-bound; moving the model to CPU will fix it.
D. The workload is limited by Python overhead; using a faster CPU will always solve it.
questions.explanation: If memory bandwidth is near peak while compute units are underutilized, the kernel is likely memory-bound: it spends time moving data rather than doing math. Common remedies include fusing ops (reduce reads/writes), using more efficient kernels, improving locality/coalescing, reducing precision (less bytes moved), or restructuring to increase arithmetic intensity (more compute per byte).

questions.question_text: In training deep neural networks, GPUs often outperform CPUs. Which explanation best captures the core reason for this on typical ML workloads?
options: A. GPUs have much higher clock speeds, so each core runs faster than CPU cores.
B. GPUs are optimized for high-throughput parallel math (SIMT) and have very high memory bandwidth, which matches tensor/matrix operation patterns.
C. GPUs have larger L1/L2 caches than CPUs, so they avoid most memory accesses.
D. GPUs are better because they run Python code faster than CPUs.
questions.explanation: Most training/inference kernels are dominated by dense linear algebra (GEMM/conv/attention) that can be expressed as many independent, similar operations. GPUs execute these efficiently using SIMT-style parallelism (many threads doing similar work) and provide high memory bandwidth to feed those arithmetic units. CPU strengths (low-latency control flow, large caches, fewer powerful cores) often matter less for these highly parallel tensor ops.
